description: Everything implies itself — even when we say it about everything.

guides.text: The goal is $\forall x.\; P(x) \to P(x)$. In words: for every $x$, $P(x)$ [implication|implies] itself. This is the identity theorem from problem 01, generalised to all possible things. To prove a universal statement, we pick an arbitrary [witness|witness] — a fresh term variable — prove the statement for that witness, then generalise.

The rule `fix` introduces a fresh term variable into the [context|context], just like `assume` does for [proposition|propositions]. It gives us $\{x\} \vdash x$ — we now have an arbitrary $x$ to work with.
Now, under the [assumption|assumption] that we have this arbitrary $x$, assume $P(x)$ — a concrete instance of the [predicate|predicate].
The two [assumption|assumptions] live in separate contexts: $\{x\}$ and $\{P(x)\}$. To use both, merge them with `and-intro`.
Extract $P(x)$ from the merged [context|context] using `and-elim-r`. Now both $x$ and $P(x)$ are in the context of this step.
[assumption|Discharge] $P(x)$ with `->-intro`. The [context|context] shrinks to just $\{x\}$ — we've proved $P(x) \to P(x)$ under the arbitrary [witness|witness] $x$.

The final move: `forall-intro` generalises over $x$. It checks that $x$ appears nowhere else free in the [context|context] (freshness) and wraps the [conclusion|conclusion]: $\forall x.\; P(x) \to P(x)$. The [witness|witness] is discharged, the proof stands for all $x$.

unlocks.name: fix
unlocks.description: Introduce a fresh term variable into the context — like assume, but for terms.

unlocks.name: ∀-intro
unlocks.description: If you prove $\varphi$ for an arbitrary $x$ not appearing elsewhere, the proof holds for all $x$. Universal generalisation.
unlocks.example:
1. (fix x)  →  {x} ⊢ x
2. (assume (App P x))  →  {x, P(x)} ⊢ P(x)
3. (->-intro (App P x) 2)  →  {x} ⊢ P(x) → P(x)
4. (forall-intro x 3)  →  ⊢ ∀x. P(x) → P(x)
